stations.label: BORN TO RANSOM A KING
stations.line: I was struck in gold to buy back Jean le Bon from the English, and so my first act was to make a king's humiliation legible in metal — franc, meaning free, though nothing about my birth was free.
stations.era_photo: A medieval mint chamber lit by tallow candles, a woman in a fitted 14th-century houppelande of deep blue and gold thread standing beside an anvil where the franc à cheval is being struck; the image rendered as an illuminated manuscript miniature, tempera and gold leaf on vellum, flat perspective and jewel-toned pigments.

stations.label: THE REPUBLIC MAKES ME DECIMAL
stations.line: The Revolution took my chaos of sols and deniers and imposed reason upon it — one hundred centimes to the franc, because a republic that has just decapitated its king cannot tolerate an incoherent coinage.
stations.era_photo: A Directory-era Parisian bureau of assignats and coin, a woman in a high-waisted white Empire chemise gown with a tricolor sash standing among stacks of freshly minted silver francs; rendered as a hand-tinted engraving of the 1790s, fine crosshatched line work with pale watercolor washes.

stations.label: I ANCHOR A CURRENCY UNION
stations.line: France, Belgium, Switzerland, Italy — they took my weight and fineness as their common law, and for the first time I was not merely a coin but an architecture, which is the only form of power I have ever respected.
stations.era_photo: A gilded Second Empire salon with heavy velvet drapes, a woman in a wide crinoline gown of bronze silk with lace at the throat seated at a marble table strewn with treaty documents; rendered as an albumen silver print, warm sepia tones, the slight softness and long-exposure stillness of 1860s photography.

stations.label: THE GENERAL RESCUES ME
stations.line: I had collapsed under war and empire and inflation, a franc that meant nothing, until de Gaulle returned and gave me a new franc worth one hundred of the old — grandeur restored not by sentiment but by arithmetic.
stations.era_photo: A stark Banque de France office in the Fifth Republic's first year, a woman in a tailored grey Dior-line suit with structured shoulders and a single strand of pearls, standing before a portrait of de Gaulle; rendered as a black-and-white silver gelatin press photograph, high contrast, the crisp documentary style of 1958 Paris-Match.

stations.label: THE NIGHT DISCIPLINE WON
stations.line: Mitterrand's socialist ambition collided with the market, and in a single grave decision we chose to stay in the European system and impose rigueur — I learned that night that one does not devalue a currency, one confesses a failure.
stations.era_photo: A nocturnal government meeting room at the Élysée, a woman in a sober navy skirt suit with the wider silhouette of the early eighties, cigarette smoke and papers, exhaustion in the eyes; rendered as a grainy color press photograph on Kodak film, the muted greens and browns of early-1980s interior lighting.

stations.label: THE SILENCE AT 3 A.M.
stations.line: When the speculators broke the sterling and turned upon me, we raised rates and did not blink, and I remember the precise quality of silence in the boardroom at three in the morning — the sound of an institution refusing to break.
stations.era_photo: A dim Banque de France boardroom during the ERM crisis, a woman in a charcoal power suit with a silk scarf, screens glowing with exchange rates behind her, coffee cups abandoned; rendered as a color 35mm photograph with the slightly cool cast and mild grain of early-1990s film stock.

stations.label: I SIGN AWAY SIX CENTURIES
stations.line: On the first of January the notes and coins vanished from French hands and the euro took my place — I did the political groundwork, absorbed the criticism, and signed the paper that ended six hundred years of monetary sovereignty, and I still do not know whether it was transcendence or erasure.
stations.era_photo: A bright Paris bank branch in winter, a woman in an elegant black wool coat and grey cashmere scarf watching francs exchanged for euros at a counter; rendered as an early digital color photograph, slightly flat dynamic range and the clean crispness of a 2002 professional DSLR.

stations.label: THE ARCHITECT REMEMBERS
stations.line: I no longer circulate, but I made Sofia Kraus possible, and I keep my Clairefontaine notebook still — for the market is a fact, the institution is a choice, and I chose to become something larger than myself.
stations.era_photo: A quiet contemporary study lined with books and framed old franc notes, a woman in a timeless tailored ivory blouse and dark trousers, silver in her hair, writing by hand in a notebook; rendered as a high-resolution modern digital photograph, natural window light, shallow depth of field, warm neutral tones.
